text: And he gave four wagons and eight oxen to the Merarite division for their work. All their work was done under the leadership of Ithamar son of Aaron the priest.

content:
Continuing from yesterday, we saw the **four wagons** God gave to the Merarites; Spirit, Truth, Grace, and Wisdom, because their burden was structural and heavy. Today we move to the **oxen**. Wagons carry the load, but oxen pull the load. Wagons are support, but oxen are strength and endurance in action. In the same way, God not only gives us provisions like Word, Spirit, grace, and wisdom. He also forms character inside us so we can keep pulling without collapsing.

We already saw four oxen qualities earlier with the Gershonites: **faithfulness, obedience, patience, and love**. Those four qualities are like the first set of oxen God forms in every servant who wants to carry holy things. But now, with the Merarites, the weight is heavier. They carried boards, sockets, pillars, and bars. So, the same qualities are not removed, but sharpened. And four more are brought into clearer view because the load is greater.

Today we will focus on the **next two oxen**; the fifth and the sixth qualities.
The fifth quality is **strength**. The Merarites needed real strength because their pieces were heavy and their responsibility was serious. Spiritually, there are times when tenderness alone is not enough. There are seasons when the heart must stand strong in Yahweh. Scripture says, “Be strong in Yahweh and in the power of His might” (Ephesians 6:10). This is not talking about loud confidence. It is talking about inner strength that comes from God. It is the strength that keeps a believer steady when pressure rises.

This kind of strength is not always shown in big moments. Sometimes it shows in daily survival. It shows in holding a family together when things are falling apart. It shows in standing for truth when people misunderstand. It shows in continuing prayer when answers seem delayed. It shows in serving quietly when there is no appreciation. This is Merarite strength. It is not the strength of pride. It is the strength of leaning on God. Paul spoke of this strength when he said, “I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me” (Philippians 4:13). Paul’s life was not easy. Yet he continued because he was strengthened from above. Strength is an ox because without strength, the load will not move.

There is also a warning here. Many believers try to carry heavy work with natural strength. They push themselves until they become bitter, tired, and empty. But God does not want His servants to break. God wants His servants to be strengthened in Him. That is why He says, “Be strong in Yahweh.” This teaches us a practical step. When the load feels heavy, we must stop depending on ourselves and begin depending on God again. Prayer must become real.

The word must become food. Fellowship must become support. We must ask God for strength, and we must receive it with humility. God does not shame those who ask. He gives to those who lean.

The sixth quality is **steadfastness**. Strength gets us moving, but steadfastness keeps us from shifting. The Merarites carried structure, and structure must stand firm. If boards lean, the house leans. If pillars move, the house shakes.

So God forms steadfastness in those who carry structural responsibility. Scripture says, “Be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of Yahweh” (1 Corinthians 15:58). This verse is like a command to be an ox.Do not be moved easily. Do not change direction every time wind blows.Do not drop the work because pressure comes.

Steadfastness is especially needed in our time. Many people start well but do not remain. Many are excited at the beginning but slow down when the work becomes repetitive or difficult. Many hold truth when it is popular but loosen it when it costs them. But God is looking for people who stand. Nehemiah is a good example. When opposition came, he did not leave the wall. He did not stop building because enemies mocked him. He stayed with the work until it was finished. That is steadfastness. In real life, steadfastness means we do not abandon prayer because we feel dry. We do not abandon holiness because temptation feels strong. We do not abandon God’s house because we feel tired. We do not abandon love because someone hurt us. We remain. We stand.
We continue.

There is also a deep encouragement in steadfastness. When we stay, God builds. When we remain, God strengthens. When we do not move away from truth, God makes the house firm. A person who is steadfast becomes a support for others. People who are weak lean on the one who stands. People who are confused are helped by the one who does not shift. This is why steadfastness is an ox. It pulls the load not only for itself but for the whole house.

So today we saw two more oxen for the Merarite burden: **strength** and **steadfastness**. Strength is the inner power God gives to carry heavy responsibility. Steadfastness is the firm stand that does not shift when pressure rises. These are not natural gifts for the flesh. They are formed by God in those who submit to Him. And this is part of the mercy of God. God does not only give burdens; He also forms the oxen within us to pull them.

Tomorrow, we will continue and study the **last two oxen**; the seventh and eighth qualities that God forms in those who carry heavy responsibility in His house.

title: Prayer
text:
Our God and Father, thank You for the strength You give for heavy work.
Teach us to be strong in you and not in our own pride.
Form steadfastness in us so we do not shift when pressure comes.
When we feel weak, remind us to lean on You again, and to receive Your help with humility.
Make us steady workers who keep pulling the holy load until the work is finished.
In Christ name, amen.
